content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 15 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 15 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 15:1 — "remind you...of the gospel I preached" / "make known to you...the good news which I proclaimed" / "remind you...of the Good News I preached"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I make known to you...the good news which I proclaimed" | Literal gnorizo — "make known" (not "remind") |
| LOGOS | "I would remind you...of the gospel I preached" | Interprets gnorizo as "remind" in this context |
| PNEUMA | "Let me now remind you...of the Good News I preached to you before" | Adds "now" and "before" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Gnorizo literally means "make known," but in context Paul is recalling what they already know (v.1b: "which you received"). LOGOS's "remind" captures the rhetorical function. PNEUMA's additions are stylistic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:2 — "unless you believed in vain" / "unless you believed in vain" / "unless, of course, you believed something that was never true in the first place"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unless you believed in vain" | Literal eike |
| LOGOS | "unless you believed in vain" | Same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "unless, of course, you believed something that was never true in the first place" | Major interpretive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/EMET's rendering. PNEUMA's expansion ("something that was never true in the first place") is one possible interpretation of eike ("in vain/without cause/groundlessly") but drastically exceeds the text's scope and imposes a specific interpretive framework.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET.

---

## 15:3 — "as of first importance" / "among [the] first [things]" / "what was most important"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "among [the] first [things]" | Literal en protois — temporal or priority ambiguity |
| LOGOS | "as of first importance" | Interprets as priority ranking |
| PNEUMA | "what was most important and what had also been passed on to me" | Amplified priority reading |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "as of first importance" (LOGOS). En protois can mean "among the first things" (temporally) or "as of first importance" (priority). Both senses may be present, but "first importance" captures the theological weight Paul assigns to this creedal formula.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:5 — "Cephas" / "Kephas" / "Peter"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Kephas" | Transliterated Aramaic form |
| LOGOS | "Cephas" | Standard English transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "Peter" | Common name for accessibility |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Cephas" (LOGOS). Paul uses Kephas in his letters; the consensus preserves this, as in 9:5.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Consistent name-rendering policy.

---

## 15:7 — "James" / "Iakobos" / "James"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Iakobos" | Transliterated Greek form |
| LOGOS | "James" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "James" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "James" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Standard anglicization of Iakobos.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 15:8 — "one untimely born" / "the [one] untimely born" / "I had been born at the wrong time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as to the [one] untimely born" | Literal ektroma with article |
| LOGOS | "as to one untimely born" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "as though I had been born at the wrong time" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "born at the wrong time" captures one dimension of ektroma but misses the violent connotation — the word means "miscarriage/abortion," implying a traumatic, abnormal birth into apostleship. "Untimely born" preserves this layered meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The violent overtone of ektroma is preserved.

---

## 15:9 — "church of God" / "assembly of God" / "God's church"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the assembly of God" | Renders ekklesia as "assembly" |
| LOGOS | "the church of God" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "God's church" | Possessive reordering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the church of God" (LOGOS). Consistent rendering of ekklesia as "church" in the consensus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:10 — "was not in vain" / "was not empty" / "not without results"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the grace of him toward me was not empty" | Literal kene — "empty" |
| LOGOS | "his grace toward me was not in vain" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "not without results" | Dynamic rendering focusing on outcome |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "was not in vain" (LOGOS). EMET's "empty" is literally accurate (kene) but "in vain" is the standard English rendering in this context. PNEUMA's "without results" is functionally equivalent but less elevated.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:14 — "in vain" / "empty" / "useless"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "empty [is] the proclamation of us, and empty also [is] the faith of you" | Literal kenon/kene |
| LOGOS | "our preaching is in vain and your faith is in vain" | Both rendered "in vain" |
| PNEUMA | "all our preaching is useless, and your faith is useless" | "Useless" — pragmatic emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in vain." PNEUMA's "useless" shifts from the concept of emptiness/futility to practical utility. Paul's point is that faith would be groundless and empty, not merely impractical.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:20 — "firstfruits" / "firstfruit" / "the first of a great harvest"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] firstfruit of the [ones] having fallen asleep" | Singular "firstfruit" |
| LOGOS | "the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep" | Plural "firstfruits" (traditional English form) |
| PNEUMA | "He is the first of a great harvest of all who have died" | Full metaphor expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "firstfruits" (LOGOS). The Greek aparche is singular but the English "firstfruits" is the established biblical term. PNEUMA's "first of a great harvest" makes the agricultural metaphor explicit, which is interpretively helpful but exceeds the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:22 — "in Adam" / "in the Adam" / "because we all belong to Adam"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the Adam all die, so also in the Christos all will be made alive" | Articles preserved |
| LOGOS | "as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "everyone dies because we all belong to Adam, everyone who belongs to Christ will be given new life" | Full paraphrase; "belong to" for "in" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The prepositional phrase "in Adam/in Christ" (en to Adam/en to Christo) is theologically loaded — it expresses corporate solidarity and participation. PNEUMA's "belong to" dilutes this participatory theology into mere affiliation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The "in" language preserves Pauline participatory theology.

---

## 15:29 — "baptized on behalf of the dead" / "baptized for the dead" / "baptized for those who are dead"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] being baptized for the dead" | Literal hyper ton nekron |
| LOGOS | "being baptized on behalf of the dead" | "On behalf of" interprets hyper |
| PNEUMA | "people being baptized for those who are dead" | Adds "people" and "those who are" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "on behalf of the dead." This is one of the NT's most obscure verses. LOGOS's "on behalf of" is a standard rendering of hyper that preserves the vicarious dimension without resolving the interpretive puzzle. EMET's "for" is more ambiguous. PNEUMA's expansion adds no clarity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:33 — "Bad company ruins good morals" / "Bad associations corrupt good morals" / "bad company corrupts good character"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Bad associations corrupt good morals" | Literal homiliai — "associations/company" |
| LOGOS | "Bad company ruins good morals" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "bad company corrupts good character" | "Character" for ethe |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. This is a quotation from the Greek poet Menander (Thais, fragment 218). "Bad company ruins good morals" is the traditional rendering. PNEUMA's "character" for ethe ("morals/character/habits") is acceptable but "morals" is more established.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:34 — "Wake up from your drunken stupor" / "Sober up righteously" / "Think carefully about what is right"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Sober up righteously and do not sin" | Literal eknepsate dikaios |
| LOGOS | "Wake up from your drunken stupor, as is right, and do not go on sinning" | Expanded metaphor |
| PNEUMA | "Think carefully about what is right, and stop sinning" | Drops the intoxication metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Eknepsate literally means "sober up/come to your senses" (from the metaphor of intoxication). LOGOS preserves the intoxication metaphor ("drunken stupor"). PNEUMA drops it entirely ("think carefully"), losing Paul's sharp rebuke.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The metaphor of intoxication is preserved.

---

## 15:36 — "You foolish person!" / "Fool!" / "What a foolish question!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Fool!" | Literal aphron — direct address |
| LOGOS | "You foolish person!" | Expanded but still direct |
| PNEUMA | "What a foolish question!" | Redirects from person to question |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "Fool!" is starkly literal. PNEUMA's shift from "foolish person" to "foolish question" softens Paul's rebuke — Paul addresses the person (aphron is a vocative), not the question.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:42 — "perishable/imperishable" / "corruption/incorruption" / "planted...raised to live forever"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It is sown in corruption, it is raised in incorruption" | Literal phthora/aphtharsia |
| LOGOS | "What is sown is perishable; what is raised is imperishable" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Our earthly bodies are planted in the ground when we die, but they will be raised to live forever" | Full paraphrase; "planted" for "sown" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "corruption/incorruption" are etymologically precise but slightly archaic. PNEUMA's expansion loses the concise antithetical structure that is one of the most powerful rhetorical features of the passage. The sown/raised parallelism must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The antithetical structure is preserved.

---

## 15:44 — "a natural body...a spiritual body" / "a natural body...a spiritual body" / "natural human bodies...spiritual bodies"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It is sown a natural body, it is raised a spiritual body" | Literal soma psychikon/pneumatikon |
| LOGOS | "It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body" | Same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "They are buried as natural human bodies, but they will be raised as spiritual bodies" | Pluralizes; adds "human" and "buried" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The singular "a natural body...a spiritual body" preserves the generic singular of the Greek. PNEUMA's pluralization and addition of "human" and "buried" are interpretive expansions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:45 — "a living being" / "a living soul" / "a living person"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a living soul" | Literal psychen zosan (Gen 2:7 LXX) |
| LOGOS | "a living being" | Modern rendering of the Genesis quote |
| PNEUMA | "a living person" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a living being" (LOGOS). This is a quotation from Genesis 2:7, and "living being" is the standard modern rendering of nephesh chayyah/psyche zosa. EMET's "living soul" preserves the traditional rendering but "soul" carries dualistic connotations in English that the Hebrew/Greek does not intend.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:47 — "a man of dust" / "earthy" / "made from the dust of the earth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The first man [is] from [the] earth, earthy" | Literal choikos — "earthy/made of earth" |
| LOGOS | "The first man was from the earth, a man of dust" | "Man of dust" — Genesis allusion |
| PNEUMA | "Adam, the first man, was made from the dust of the earth" | Names Adam; expands the description |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "a man of dust." This preserves the Genesis 2:7 allusion (God formed man from the dust of the ground). EMET's "earthy" is archaic. PNEUMA's naming of "Adam" makes explicit what Paul leaves as an epithet.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:51 — "Behold!" / "Behold" / "let me reveal to you a wonderful secret"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, I tell you a mystery" | Literal idou |
| LOGOS | "Behold! I tell you a mystery" | Same with exclamation |
| PNEUMA | "But let me reveal to you a wonderful secret" | "Reveal" for "tell"; "wonderful secret" for "mystery" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "wonderful secret" changes the register of mysterion, which in Paul means a divine plan previously hidden but now revealed, not simply a "wonderful secret."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:52 — "the twinkling of an eye" / "[the] blink of an eye" / "the blink of an eye"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in [the] blink of an eye" | Literal rhipe ophthalmou |
| LOGOS | "in the twinkling of an eye" | Traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "in the blink of an eye" | Modern equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "in the twinkling of an eye" (LOGOS). The traditional rendering has become proverbial in English and should be preserved for its liturgical and literary resonance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Traditional rendering preserved.

---

## 15:55 — "O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?" — textual variant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Where, O death, [is] the victory of you? Where, O death, [is] the sting of you?" | Follows Sinaiticus: "death" in both lines |
| LOGOS | "O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?" | Same reading, smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three follow the Sinaiticus reading with "death" (thanate) in both lines, rather than "Hades" (hade) in the second line as in some manuscripts. LOGOS/PNEUMA's English rendering is adopted. Unanimous on the textual reading; minor stylistic variation only.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Sinaiticus reading unanimous.

---

## 15:58 — "beloved brothers" / "beloved brothers of me" / "dear brothers and sisters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "beloved brothers of me" | Hyper-literal with possessive |
| LOGOS | "my beloved brothers" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "my dear brothers and sisters" | Adds "and sisters"; uses "dear" for "beloved" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "my beloved brothers." "Beloved" is stronger than "dear" and preserves the elevated register. PNEUMA's "and sisters" is a gender-inclusive expansion common in modern translations but not present in the Greek adelphoi.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion | 10 | Expanded metaphors, added "Adam," "wonderful secret," "and sisters," paraphrased antithetical structures |
| EMET literalism/archaism | 6 | "Earthy," "corruption/incorruption," "Fool!," transliterated names |
| Rhetorical structure preservation | 3 | Sown/raised parallelism (vv.42-44), "in Adam/in Christ" (v.22), "Behold" (v.51) |
| Traditional rendering preference | 2 | "Twinkling of an eye," "Bad company ruins good morals" |
| Theological precision | 3 | "In Adam" participatory language, "firstfruits," "mystery" |
| Name rendering | 2 | Cephas/Peter, James/Iakobos |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 15 is Paul's most sustained theological argument, and the disagreements reflect the tension between preserving Paul's rhetorical structures (the antithetical pairs of vv.42-44, the creedal formula of vv.3-5, the taunt-song of vv.54-55) and making them accessible. PNEUMA's tendency to expand and paraphrase was particularly problematic in the resurrection-body passage (vv.42-44), where the concise antithetical structure is one of Paul's most powerful literary achievements. The consensus consistently preserved these structures while updating archaic vocabulary (LOGOS's "perishable/imperishable" over EMET's "corruption/incorruption").

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
